sentences.text: My name is Bronia Hatfield, B-R-O-N for Nelly, I-A.
Hatfield, H-A-T for Tom, F-I-E-L-D.
Bronia-- Broniaslava Goldstein, Goldstein, 'Gold-stine' actually in Poland.
But it's spelled in Australia G-O-L-D-S-T-E-I-N.
No.
Well, if we consider the full name is Broniaslava, then my name-- nickname or name I'm known as is Bronia.
Or Bronka.
I was born in Lublin in Poland.
L-U-B-L-I-N.
On the 4th of November, 1924.
70, exactly. 70.
We lived in an apartment in the center of town in the [POLISH], in [POLISH].
We had a small apartment there.
That-- these are my earliest memories that I have.

And my father was never there, because my father left Lublin and went-- had a business in eastern Poland in Volyn, in a place called Lutsk, in a small town called Lutsk, which, by the way, was the capital city of Volynia.
The whole area of eastern Poland was called Volyn and Lutsk was at the time the biggest city in Volyn.
So that meant that in Lublin, there was my mother, my sister Lola, and myself.
And there was usually a live-in woman who cooked and washed and look-- also took me to the park.
These are my childhood memories, that I went always with this Polish woman, usually, Christian woman.
I only remember one, so she must have been with us for a long time.
And we went to-- she used to take me to the park every day before I started the kindergarten.
Right.
And then, you went to a kindergarten in Lublin?
Then, I went to a kindergarten, Mrs.
Friedman's-- [POLISH] Mrs.
Friedman's kindergarten, which was a private kindergarten for I would say select children.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a small kindergarten, small kindergarten.
I think they were the well-off families, really, children of solicitors and doctors and a few business people.
And my father was a businessman.
But strange enough, I went-- it's not strange.
But anyway, that's where my sister went and that's where I went.
And then, we left Lublin.
My sister left the kindergarten.
And she enrolled-- she was enrolled at the private school.
But then, we left, anyway, and we went to Lutsk.
We joined our father in Lutsk.
My father had a mill in Lutsk.
And he also had-- we had houses there.
Anyway, we came there.
And when I arrived there with my patents, with my mother and my sister, it was a different life.
It was life in a house with a garden, with pets, with neighbors, whereas in Lublin, it was the middle of town and out of the window, you could only see the street and the high court across the road.
I think it was in the very early '30s or maybe very late '20s.
I-- I really-- I've never really stopped to think about it.
It must've been maybe 1930.
I was about six years old.
Yeah.
And your sister Lola was about eight.
That's right.
And when-- I went to public school, public primary school in Lutsk, and then to a public high school when the-- just before the war broke out.
I remember the greatest joy of being in the park and watching-- oh, I always block on that name, the-- [POLISH] the little thing that run up a tree with a mesh--
Squirrels.
This park was full of squirrels in that-- in Lublin.
And I-- I loved watching the squirrels and watching nature and walking amongst the most beautiful trees.
There were a lot of-- [POLISH]-- chestnut trees, a lot of chestnut-- chestnut trees.
And it was really beautiful, that park, really lovely.
I also remember I had girlfriends and boys from kindergarten, of course, friends.
I got on extremely well with my sister, Lola.
In fact, we never argued, never.
Until she got killed later on in Lutsk, we have never had a row and a quarrel in our lives.
And we shared the bedroom.
We shared the bedroom, which had two single beds, a wardrobe, and a desk.
And there was never a fight.
I was the one who occupied the desk, later on, of course.
But anyway, just to tell you that I had a wonderful companion in my sister and we were very, very harmonious.
We were very-- we sort of-- we-- it was a natural harmony.
We didn't have to work at it.
It was there all the time.
She was a little bit more mild than I was.
I think I was a little bit maybe more affirmative, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe a little bit more determined.
And she was-- she was a little bit more placid, compared with me.
I was-- I took after my father and I was rather impulsive and very-- not very, but excitable and-- and emotional.
And she was much less so.
So the harmony was there.
Yes, yes indeed.
In fact, it was said that Lola took after the Nismans, because my mother's maiden name was Nisman, and I took after my father.
In fact, I-- I had my father's build.
My mother was tall and slim, and my father was middle-size, you know.
He was just a fraction, maybe one inch shorter than my mother.
But I took after my father.
No.
Well, I-- I remember one thing, that we had a caretaker in the building and his daughter went crazy.
And I remember she was locked up in a cellar which had doors, open doors.
They were only like in a zoo, the crates, metal crates.
And this girl cried and screamed.
She obviously must-- now looking back, she must've been a schizophrenic.

But it was a terrific, terribly traumatic experience to me to see that they lock up a human being who's in pain and allow this girl, summer or winter-- no, I think it took maybe six months for her, a matter of months for her being there and they removed her, obviously, somewhere else.
But this left such a mark on me, how unfair of the parents to lock up a young girl who is in pain and listen to her scream and do nothing about it.
Well, I coped with it.
It was painful but-- it was painful, of course, but I had no choice.
And don't forget that I went already to the kindergarten, probably.
So I only had it weekends, maybe.
But in any case, I remember this as a very traumatic experience.
Yes.
My father used to come and visit us very often.
But then eventually, of course, we packed our household and we went and joined him in Lutsk and he bought a house there.
Originally, we lived in a house by the mill.
There were three other families living there.
It was a very big house and we had a quarter of the house with a separate entrance, of course.
But then very soon after, my father bought a beautiful house with-- there was-- there were people living there with three sons and they had an enormous tennis court.
But of course, my father didn't believe in two girls playing tennis.
In those days in Poland, that was not in fashion.
So the tennis court was cemented over and we ride our-- we rode our bicycles around it and grew-- had a little veggie patch, Lola and I, we looked after.
But that was later in  Lutsk.
Oh.
Also from Lublin-- yes.
OK.
In Lutsk, [SIGHS] well, I was always top of the class.
I was always the best in every subject, except for gymnastics, where I was very average, very average.
But everything else, you name it and I was tops.
And I think that some of the kids were jealous of me.
First of all, I was Jewish and they did not admit Jews to this high school.
You had to be Christian and you had to-- and you had to-- I don't know.
You had to be bright in order-- well, I don't know.
Maybe the Christians got in easily.
There were only a few Jewish children in the whole school.
And the school would have had, oh, many hundred of-- hundreds of children, probably 600 children or so.
But there were only-- in my class, there were only three Jewish children, three girls.
In other classes, there could have been one or two.
So-- and I'll just give you-- illuminate-- of course, all of them knew that I'm the best, because I was always first.
I got always the best marks.

And if the new subject-- if a new subject was introduced, even in mathematics, I was called to the blackboard to-- to hear my opinion what I think about it before the teacher actually taught the news to the class.
So-- but why-- I'll illustrate to you.
I had a girlfriend there, a Polish girl, Danka Cobretska.
She was my best girlfriend.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day-- we had lockers at school.
Every child had a locker.
One day, I came to my locker and outside on the locker-- we put our-- our coats there and umbrellas, whatever.
And one day, I came to my locker and outside the locker was written down 'Black Pearl.' And I remember when I saw it, I cried.
It's OK, I was a pearl.
I was-- I was something good.
But I had to be black, because I was Jewish.
So somebody wanted to hurt me.
So instead of putting-- oh, you know, they didn't have to put anything.
But 'Black Pearl'-- we never knew that black pearls existed in those days.
So that was sort of an insult that was to hurt me.
So in--in situations like these, of course, I felt discriminated against.
But otherwise, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took it for granted that being Jewish, I'll be persecuted.
Mind you, there was a Jewish high school there.
There was a Jewish primary school.
But my parents chose for me to go to-- to the public schools.
Even as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] inaccessible as they were, my parents-- I don't know whether they thought that the tuition was better there or that the chances in life are after the schooling better.
I have no idea.
But my sister went to private schools and I went to the-- to the public.
Not Jewish.
Private Polish school.
There was a private Polish high school, public Polish high school, and a Jewish high school.
And the Ukrainian high school.
And I don't think there was a Russian one.
But there definitely was a Ukrainian high school.
I think they-- my parents were fairly assimilated.
They were the average well-to-do, well middle-class Jewish family.
We had a comfortable house.
We had-- my father had a very good position.
He was very respected.
And my mother was a lady of leisure, but very serious person.
They read a lot, and you know-- so I don't know.
But they-- they-- it was a very traditional Jewish home.
The Jewish holidays were kept very-- very diligently.
In fact, for a seder, there was always a chap invited who wore payos from the yeshiva.
So every Pesach at seder time, there was a yeshiva boy sitting with us when my father was conducting the seder.
And-- but every year, there was a different yeshiva boy, not the same one.
Well, I'm not sure about that.
I think it was, basically.
My mother didn't touch anything treif, but my father did.
I know he ate outside of the house.
And also, we had ham, my sister and I, for breakfast, and my father, too.
So it wasn't kosher, but my mother didn't touch it.
It was a kosher home until the war broke out.
It was a few exceptions, you know, special plates for the ham, for instance.
No.
I can't recall.
I can't recall that.
But I know that during the holidays, we went to-- to shul.
My father had a seat in the shul.
My father was a very good mixer.
My father mixed with-- he had friends amongst the non-Jews.
And my mother, I think-- they went-- at night, they were not at home.
So where they went, I don't know.
I was in my bedroom studying or going visiting a girlfriend or whatever.
I didn't know where they were.
They went visiting friends, obviously, but I don't know.
And my father's friends used to come and-- men, and they used to discuss politics and stuff like that.
So this I remember vividly.
But I don't-- I don't think they were so terribly social, going to nightclubs and stuff like that.
They went a few times.
But it was a rarity more than a-- than a routine [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] activity.
I can't tell you.
I don't know.
I know that we were felt-- of course, we never felt fully as a full citizen, never.
There was always the threat that someone might come.
But I-- I-- I didn't ponder over it, strange enough.
We didn't-- we took it for granted that we are different, that we are a minority, that we-- that we're supposed to suffer for being a minority, for being Jewish.
But somehow or other-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, I had Polish friends, of course.
I had a lot.
In fact, the police-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the headquarters of the police were two houses away from-- from us.
And the commander's daughter, Hanka, went to the same class as I.
So I lived practically in the-- in the commander's or the chief of police's house with Hanka and she came to our place.
So, I mean, that was one.
And there was Vanda and there was this one, I mean, the girlfriends constantly-- the Polish girlfriends, Christian girlfriends, because I went to school with them.
So that was a natural thing that we mix together.
But of course, on the side, I had Jewish girlfriends, also the ones that went to the high school.
People somehow or other stuck-- the children stuck to the schools they went to.
And the boys, there were Jewish boys, too, in the private schools, in the private high school.
And they-- they were courting my sister and me and they were coming and visiting us.
And it was very interesting.
The Jewish boys and the girls were one kind of crowd and the Christian ones was another crowd.
We didn't have them at the same time, so to speak.
Very easily, very happily.
I did.
I did.
Amongst-- yes, I did.
But it actually happened after the war broke out.
It actually happened when the war broke out that another girl called Vanda-- there were three sisters.
Her mother worked in the bank.
And this girl hurt me very badly, very badly.
When the war broke out, she wouldn't talk to me.
But then again, look, the war broke out in '39.
The Russians walked in.
When the Russians walked in, we were kicked out of our house.
Fortunately, my father and his-- his brother Leon and a few other people, they just finished the construction of a house which had six units in it, six separate apartments, units.
And we-- when the war broke out, it was just finished.
So when we lived in the big house with the tennis court that became a courtyard, we moved out.
We had to move out, because my father was well-off.
And a lot of people in the area were poor, so we were-- and there was a Communist regime arriving.
So my father-- we were immediately expelled from the house.
But fortunately, we could go to that unit there in the city.
But shortly after, we were kicked out from the city.
We couldn't stay.
We got Paragraph 13, which meant that we were too rich to be tolerated in Lutsk and allowed to live in Lutsk.
We had to leave.
So my father was-- actually, they tried to deport my father to Siberia.
He was on a truck going to the railway station.
He jumped off the truck and went into hiding.
He knocked out, I think, a tooth.
And he went to hiding somewhere under-- in a small village somewhere-- I don't know how many kilometers-- in the Olyka area.
It would have been about, I don't know, 70 kilometers or so away from Lutsk.
That was in 1939.
The Russians.
And then, in 1940, we moved out.
No, we left in 1940.
We lived in that unit for a while.
We lived in that unit for a while.

And in fact, they put a Soviet-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Soviet-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] employee for the Soviet government, some high-up family, a chap with his wife and a child, because we had three rooms, two bedrooms and a-- we had two bedrooms.
Yes.
I shared one with my sister and my parents had one, and a big sitting room.
So we had to give them one of our-- one of our bedrooms.
The big bedroom we had to give to the Soviet family, so that my parents took over the sitting room as a bedroom and I and Lola stayed in our bedroom.
So we'd never had a proper apartment already.
Since we left the house, we hadn't had an apartment to ourselves.
Another thing I remember from childhood-- from this area is that-- this era is when the Russians came in, a priest stayed with us for a while before they came in.
And he hanged himself in our bathroom.
So when I arrived from school, I was not allowed into the bathroom because he was hanging there.
He was afraid that either when the Germans come in or the Russians come in-- we were bombed.
And there were curtains.
The lights-- they were-- shutters had to be down so that the bombers didn't see the city.
And he was afraid that-- that he will get killed.
I don't know.
I don't know why he killed himself.
I don't know.
I was a child.
I was very young and I hardly knew him.
But I know that he hanged himself in our bathroom, a priest.
Anyway, I didn't know him well.
He was just passing.
He was probably running from the Germans to the Soviets or vice versa.
I don't know.
My parents befriended him, I would imagine.
They probably befriended him.
He was-- probably had nowhere to stay.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So then the Russians came.
And this was very bad.
This was very bad.
Well, I didn't feel it, because I went to school.
And I played the piano very well.
And under the Russians, the teachers were marvelous in the conservatorium there, which I don't think they called it a conservatorium.
In any case, I could develop that skill.
And it was somehow or other-- but I could never win, because, you see, when the Russians came, I was the rich one.
I was the persecuted one for another reason, not for being Jewish, but for being-- having been from a well-to-do family.
So I was always sort of on the [LAUGHS] wrong side, so to speak.
But somehow or other, it never stopped me.
I was so determined to be happy and to-- and I was-- and to-- to thrive and to have friends and to have interests.
And I really had a good life.
And then, the Germans came.
Well, I'll tell you.
When the Germans-- when the Russians were coming in and we heard the roar of the-- of the tanks and the roar of the crowd, we stood on the balcony, my father, my sister, and I.
And my father was saying, well, this is the end of our lives.
My father survived the First World War in Vienna.
He was very fond of the Austrians, of the Germans.
He spoke fluent German.
He was young.
He was a young Polish Jew who had a brother, a very successful banker, in Vienna, Uncle Adolph, who was the eldest in the family, and my father was the youngest.
There was, I don't know, a very large age difference between them.
And my father stayed, lived in Vienna.
Until he-- until after the war, the First World War, he came to Poland and in the early '20s met my mother, and they married in Lublin.
And my sister was born and then I was born.
Anyway, I remember this, standing on the balcony, my father saying, this is the end of our lives.
Now we're not going to have any life anymore.
My father was very anti-Communist from the start.

And the-- I want to tell you that he-- we moved from the house because one of the reasons we had to move is my father was afraid the Communists-- the people who turned Communist with the entrance of the Communist regime would turn against him because he was well-off.
He was afraid that they'll take the revenge on him because they were poor before the war.
There was an element of Polish people and Jews who turned Communist, who were poor before the war.
And all of a sudden, they decided, this is our time now.
We are going to be the big bosses.
We are going to set the rules.
And I think my father was afraid that this element could harm him.
Yes.
He told me.
I just remember vaguely that-- I remember that on the balcony, my father was saying, look, this is the end of our lives as we knew it.
Now we're going to have a different life and it won't be as secure as it was before.
He was, in other words, afraid of the Soviet regime, very afraid, and the-- all the complications that could come with it.
Well, I had-- I had an argument with him.
You see, the point is that we had to leave Lutsk soon after.
We had to leave that apartment, that unit.
And my mother moved to Olyka.
My father moved to-- also to Olyka Station, which was about 10 kilometers, 15 kilometers away from where my mother was.
So he-- and he became director of a tar factory.
Under the Russians.
Under the Russians.
It's already '40, '41.
That's already '40, 1940.
Oh.
From memory, about 70 kilometers, 60 kilometers, from memory.
And my sister went to a village and she was teaching in a Ukrainian school.
She-- I don't think-- she may have just finished the 10 years.
In other words, she would have been 16.
She would have been 17.
She would have finished the school at about, what? 10 years from-- Anyway, she would have been about 17, 17 at the time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 17.
She was 17 at the time.
And she was teaching high-- school, school, primary school, somewhere in a-- in a village, in the Ukrainian village.
And I was in Kovel because my mother's sister lived in Kovel.
She was married to a fellow.
My mother's sister was much older than my mother.
And I was-- I went to-- they sent me to Kovel.
And-- and they put me up with some widow.
And I attended school, of course, high school.
And then, I went home in June to Olyka to see my mother.
And in a few days, the war broke out.
It was on the 21st, I think, of June '41 that the war broke out-- that the Germans marched into Poland.
In the-- but Germans walked into our part--
Yes, yes.
Into your town.
Into our part, into eastern Poland, which at time-- the Volyn, yes, which used to be Poland before the war.
And during the war in '39, the Bug River divided Poland from USSR.
And we were beyond the Bug River, so we were in the USSR part of it.
In June '41, the Germans went through the Bug River and invaded-- invaded this part of Poland and kept on going to Stalingrad, as we very well know.
Well, I saw them at Olyka.
I saw them at Olyka.
I went for a holiday to-- the schools finished in June.
I took a train, went to Olyka, and a Ukrainian boy was also going from Kovel to Olyka.
So there was a Ukrainian boy whom I never saw in my life before and me.
And I was carrying a suitcase.
He helped me.
I forgot that my father was at the station.
My father lived in a house near to the railway station.
I could have gone there.
I forgot that he was there, because I was involved in a conversation with him and we were talking, two young children talking together.
And he was going by foot to Olyka.
So I went with him to Olyka and he helped me to carry my suitcase.
And this was a few days before the Germans entered Olyka-- a few days before the Germans entered Olyka.
When they entered Olyka, oh, [SIGHS] there were two spies walking along our house, along the street, and I knew that they didn't belong there.
And I ran into the-- ran into the house and said, Mama, there are-- there are two spies here.
They don't belong.
They were looking at the houses and peeping their heads into-- because they were going-- the following day, they bombed Olyka.
So there were two German spies on a-- what's the word for it?
They were doing a-- in Polish, [POLISH].
They were-- Like a rickey?
Reconnaissance.
Yes.
They were sort of--
Surveying.
Surveying.
And the following day, the bombs came.
And really, you know, the bomb fell in the house next door and it hit an old woman who was distantly related-- I don't know how-- and she-- she got-- she got killed.
And we were in the cellar.
This is where I had my first, and only, thank God, I think, near-death experience.
And I was very young.
We were like sardines under the house in the cellar--
In hiding when the-- when they were bombing.
And the bomb fell next door that felt as if it was falling on us.
And this was a real near-death experience.
I thought this was really the end.
But strange enough, we all survived.
And what happened was the following day after the bombing, the Germans, the German tanks came in, and they-- they were-- they were-- I saw them coming.
I saw them coming.
I saw them coming.
It was a terrible sight, the blond, beautiful-looking guys with the swastikas on their uniforms [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hitting people, hitting people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was terrible.
It was terrible.
And then, of course, we had to leave Olyka.
